skill.name: go-rod-master
skill.description: Guide for Go browser automation with Rod, including scraping, Chrome DevTools Protocol workflows, request interception, stealth pages, screenshots, and concurrency patterns.
skill.summary: Build Go browser automation and scraping workflows with Rod and Chrome DevTools Protocol.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-risk alerts, but most are false positives from Markdown backticks, Go import blocks, API names, screenshots, and ordinary URLs. The confirmed concern is dual-use browser automation guidance: the skill teaches stealth anti-bot behavior, proxy configuration, request interception, response modification, JavaScript evaluation, downloads, and screenshots. No evidence of malware, credential exfiltration, destructive actions, or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use stealth automation guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly recommends stealth pages and anti-bot evasion for production scraping. This can support legitimate testing, but it can also help bypass site defenses and terms of service controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file content directly describes stealth anti-detection behavior and recommends it for real-world sites. The capability is dual-use rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Request interception and response modification examples
medium_findings.description: The examples teach intercepting browser requests, changing headers, blocking resources, and modifying JavaScript responses. These are valid testing techniques but can be abused for traffic manipulation or unauthorized instrumentation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The request hijacking behavior is explicit and runnable in the example. The sample target is benign, and no exfiltration endpoint is present.

medium_findings.title: Proxy and certificate-error bypass guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes proxy configuration, SOCKS proxy usage, proxy authentication, and ignoring SSL certificate errors. This is useful for controlled debugging but raises monitoring and interception risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The proxy and certificate-error guidance is directly present in the skill. The context is browser automation, so legitimate use is possible.

low_findings.title: Static command-execution alerts are Markdown/API false positives
low_findings.description: The numerous backtick alerts in the Markdown reference are API names and formatted code examples, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. The installation section shows user-run Go commands but does not auto-execute them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are documentation tables or fenced examples. There is no code path in the skill that executes shell commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Static dynamic-import alerts are Go import blocks
low_findings.description: The dynamic import alerts point to normal Go import declarations and a documented Go import snippet. They are not dynamic runtime imports or script loading behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is standard Go import syntax. No dynamic import expression exists in these locations.

low_findings.title: Screen capture and keylogger alerts are not confirmed
low_findings.description: Screenshot examples save local image files, and the keylogger alert comes from guidance about human-like keystroke delays. No upload routine, keystroke capture loop, or credential collection is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show local screenshot output and input simulation advice. I did not find evidence of screen capture upload or keylogging.

low_findings.title: Weak-cryptography alerts are identifier matches, not crypto use
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match substrings in Markdown, API names, or front matter. No hashing, encryption, password storage, or cryptographic implementation is present in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant context is documentation for Rod methods and metadata. I did not find cryptographic code in the flagged areas.

dangerous_patterns.title: Stealth JavaScript injection
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates injecting stealth JavaScript before page scripts run to hide automation signals.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and security-relevant because it changes browser fingerprinting behavior. It is presented as a scraping technique, not malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Response body modification through request hijacking
dangerous_patterns.description: The request hijacking example appends content to JavaScript responses and changes outbound request headers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly modifies headers and response bodies. The example is local browser automation and does not include data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Ignoring certificate errors for proxy workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The proxy guidance includes disabling certificate validation errors, which can hide transport security problems during automation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is visible in context and can be risky if copied into production. It may be legitimate for controlled MITM debugging.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct JavaScript evaluation in pages
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches direct JavaScript evaluation against pages and elements. This is common in browser automation but unsafe when scripts include untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples use static JavaScript snippets, so no injection vulnerability is shown. The pattern is still worth surfacing because users may adapt it with untrusted input.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Workflows with Go Rod
content.value_statement: Dynamic websites are difficult to scrape and test with simple HTTP clients. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code build Go Rod browser automation workflows with timeouts, stealth pages, request interception, and concurrency.
actual_capabilities: Explains Rod browser, page, element, timeout, selector, and error-handling patterns.
Shows Go examples for scraping page titles, links, search results, and dynamic content.
Demonstrates stealth page creation with go-rod/stealth and browser fingerprint checks.
Covers request interception, header modification, response changes, and network event logging.
Provides concurrency patterns with Rod page pools and isolated incognito contexts.
Documents screenshots, PDF export, file download handling, proxies, and Chrome launcher options.
limitations: It is documentation and examples, not a packaged Go library or command-line tool.
It requires a Chromium-compatible browser and does not support Firefox or Safari workflows.
It does not solve CAPTCHAs or guarantee success against strict bot protection systems.
Users must ensure scraping, interception, and stealth automation comply with site rules and law.

use_cases.title: Scrape Dynamic Product Pages
use_cases.description: Use Rod to render JavaScript-heavy pages, wait for stable DOM content, and extract structured page data.

use_cases.title: Test Browser Workflows in Go
use_cases.description: Automate form input, clicks, downloads, screenshots, and PDF checks with Go-based browser scripts.

use_cases.title: Debug Network Behavior
use_cases.description: Inspect requests, block assets, change test headers, and observe network events during browser sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Scraper
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one dynamic page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the go-rod-master skill to write a simple Go Rod scraper for this URL. Extract the page title and the main heading, use clear timeout handling, and explain how to run it.

prompt_templates.title: Add Reliable Element Waiting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the go-rod-master skill to improve my Rod script. Replace fixed sleeps with Rod wait methods, add error-returning API usage, and make selectors more reliable.

prompt_templates.title: Build Concurrent Page Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the go-rod-master skill to design a concurrent scraper for a list of URLs. Use a page pool, limit concurrency, isolate sessions, collect results safely, and include cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Review Interception and Stealth Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the go-rod-master skill to review this Rod automation plan. Identify where stealth, proxies, request interception, screenshots, or downloads create compliance or security risks.

output_examples.input: Create a Rod scraper for a JavaScript-rendered catalog page.
output: A Go workflow that launches Chromium, sets a timeout, opens the page, waits for stable content, extracts selected fields, and closes the browser.
Notes about selector choices, error handling, and when to use page search across iframes or shadow DOM.

output_examples.input: Explain how to intercept requests during a browser test.
output: A step-by-step explanation of creating a hijack router, adding URL patterns, loading responses, changing test headers, and stopping the router.
A warning to avoid capturing secrets and to run interception only in authorized test environments.

output_examples.input: Make a scraper more reliable under load.
output: Recommendations for page pooling, incognito contexts, bounded concurrency, explicit timeouts, and cleanup of browsers and pages.
A short checklist for monitoring memory use and avoiding unlimited browser creation.

best_practices: Use explicit timeouts, cleanup calls, and error-returning Rod APIs for production workflows.
Limit concurrency with page pools and reuse browser instances instead of launching one browser per task.
Use stealth, proxies, interception, screenshots, and downloads only on systems where you have authorization.
anti_patterns: Do not use fixed sleep delays when Rod wait methods can observe page or request state.
Do not copy stealth or certificate-error bypass settings into production without compliance review.
Do not log, store, or forward intercepted credentials, cookies, tokens, or private page content.

faq.question: What is Go Rod?
faq.answer: Go Rod is a Go browser automation library that controls Chromium through the Chrome DevTools Protocol.

faq.question: Does this skill install Rod automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users still run Go commands and manage dependencies in their own project.

faq.question: Can it scrape JavaScript-heavy pages?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show launching Chromium, waiting for page stability, and reading rendered DOM content.

faq.question: Does it include stealth automation?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents go-rod/stealth and explains anti-detection behavior, so use it only where automation is allowed.

faq.question: Can it intercept network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Rod request hijacking for blocking resources, changing test headers, and inspecting responses.

faq.question: Is it safe to use in production?
faq.answer: It can be used safely for authorized automation when timeouts, cleanup, rate limits, and compliance controls are applied.
